content.problemStatement:
Let **p** be a permutation of integers **1, ..., N**. Lets call **p** good if there is at least one pair of indices **(i,j)** such that **i < j** and **p[i] > j, p[j] > i**.



**p****1, ..., N****M****i (1 ≤ i ≤ M)****p(Xi)****Vi**

**M****2000000011**
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and M, where N is the number of elements in the permutation and M is the number of queries. The second line contains M queries, each query being a pair of integers Xi and Vi.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output 1 if there exists at least one pair of indices (i,j) such that i < j and p[i] > j, p[j] > i, otherwise output 0.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, there is one pair of indices (1,2) such that p[1] > 2 and p[2] > 1, so the output is 1. For the second test case, there is no such pair, so the output is 0.
For this test case, there is one pair of indices (1,2) such that p[1] > 2 and p[2] > 1, so the output is 1.
For this test case, there is one pair of indices (1,2) such that p[1] > 2 and p[2] > 1, so the output is 1.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a brute force approach by generating all possible permutations of the numbers from 1 to N and checking each permutation to see if it satisfies the condition. However, this approach is not efficient for large values of N. A more efficient approach is to use a mathematical formula to calculate the number of permutations that satisfy the condition. We can use the principle of inclusion-exclusion to calculate the number of permutations that satisfy the condition.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * M) — the time complexity is linear with respect to the number of test cases and the number of queries.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — the space complexity is constant as we only need to store a few variables.
